docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The ACR White Paper (2017) provides guidance on managing incidentally detected adrenal masses. The goal is to differentiate benign adenomas from malignancies (metastasis, adrenocortical carcinoma) and functioning tumors.

docs.title: 1. Lesion < 1 cm
docs.text:
• Recommendation: Benign, no follow-up needed.
  • Exceptions: If known malignancy, consider follow-up.

docs.title: 2. Lesion 1 - 4 cm
docs.text:
A. Benign Features (Diagnostic) - Features: unenhanced CT < 10 HU. - Management: No further follow-up.

B. Indeterminate Features (> 10 HU) - No history of cancer: - Consider Adrenal Washout CT or MRI (Chemical Shift). - If benign (adenoma): No follow-up. - If indeterminate on washout/MRI: Follow-up CT/MRI at 6-12 months. - If stable for 1 year: Benign, stop follow-up. - History of cancer: - Consider Adrenal Washout CT, MRI, or PET/CT. - If not an adenoma (or PET avid): Consider biopsy or resection.

docs.title: 3. Lesion > 4 cm
docs.text:
• Management: Resection is often recommended due to risk of Adrenocortical Carcinoma (ACC).
  • Workup: Biochemical evaluation (rule out pheochromocytoma) before biopsy or surgery. Do NOT biopsy a potentially resectable ACC (risk of seeding).

docs.title: Biochemical Evaluation
docs.text:
• All incidentalomas should arguably undergo screening for hyperfunction (Cushing's, Pheochromocytoma, Hyperaldosteronism), especially if > 1 cm.
  • Standard Screen: Plasma metanephrines (pheo), 1mg overnight dexamethasone suppression (Cushing's).

docs.title: Source
docs.text: Mayo-Smith WW, et al. Management of Incidental Adrenal Masses: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee. J Am Coll Radiol. 2017;14(8):1038-1044.

docs.title: ACR BI-RADS — Breast Imaging Reporting & Data System
docs.text:
Reference Information

Full Name: Breast Imaging Reporting and Data System Version: 5th Edition (2013) Publisher: American College of Radiology (ACR) Application: Mammography, Breast Ultrasound, Breast MRI Last Institutional Review: January 2024 Modifications: None

BI-RADS Quick Reference Category Assessment Malignancy Risk Action BI-RADS 0 Incomplete N/A Additional imaging needed BI-RADS 1 Negative Essentially 0% Routine screening BI-RADS 2 Benign Essentially 0% Routine screening BI-RADS 3 Probably benign ≤2% 6-month short-interval follow-up BI-RADS 4 Suspicious 2–95% Tissue sampling (biopsy) BI-RADS 4A Low suspicion >2% to ≤10% Biopsy BI-RADS 4B Moderate suspicion >10% to ≤50% Biopsy BI-RADS 4C High suspicion >50% to <95% Biopsy BI-RADS 5 Highly suggestive of malignancy ≥95% Biopsy BI-RADS 6 Known biopsy-proven malignancy N/A Surgical management

docs.title: Detailed Classification
docs.text:
• Category 0: Incomplete

    Additional Imaging Needed

    Evaluation is incomplete. Additional imaging is necessary before a final category can be assigned.

    Common reasons:

    • Callback from screening mammography
    • Need for additional views (spot compression, magnification)
    • Prior study comparison needed
    • Ultrasound or MRI correlation required

    Management: Complete workup before final assessment

  • Category 1: Negative

    Routine Screening

    No abnormality found. Breast tissue is symmetric. No masses, architectural distortion, or suspicious calcifications.

    Management: Routine screening interval Malignancy Risk: Essentially 0%

  • Category 2: Benign

    Routine Screening

    Definite benign finding(s). The radiologist may describe a benign finding while still concluding there is no mammographic evidence of malignancy.

    Examples:

    • Involuting, calcified fibroadenoma
    • Multiple secretory calcifications
    • Oil cysts, lipomas, galactoceles, hamartomas
    • Simple cysts on ultrasound
    • Implants

    Management: Routine screening interval Malignancy Risk: Essentially 0%

  • Category 3: Probably Benign

    Short-Interval Follow-Up

    Finding has high probability of being benign. Not expected to change over the follow-up interval, but the radiologist would prefer to establish stability.

    Qualifying lesions:

    • Mammography: Non-calcified circumscribed solid mass, focal asymmetry, solitary group of punctate calcifications
    • Ultrasound: Complicated cyst, clustered microcysts, circumscribed solid mass with benign features
    • MRI: Non-mass enhancement with kinetics suggestive of benign etiology

    Management: Initial 6-month follow-up, then 12-month × 2 years Malignancy Risk: ≤2%

  • Category 4: Suspicious

    Tissue Sampling Recommended

    Finding does not have classic benign morphology. Biopsy should be considered.

    Subcategories:

    • 4A: Low suspicion — e.g., palpable solitary, well-defined solid mass on US; single dilated duct
    • 4B: Moderate suspicion — e.g., partially circumscribed mass with ill-defined margin
    • 4C: High suspicion — e.g., irregular, hypoechoic mass with angular margins; new cluster of fine pleomorphic calcifications

    Subcategory Guidance

    Subcategorizing (4A/4B/4C) is recommended as it helps manage patient/clinician expectations for biopsy results.

    Management: Biopsy Malignancy Risk: 4A: >2–10% | 4B: >10–50% | 4C: >50–<95%

  • Category 5: Highly Suggestive of Malignancy

    Biopsy Required

    Finding has a high probability (≥95%) of being malignant. Biopsy is required before any treatment.

    Classic features:

    • Spiculated high-density mass
    • Segmental/linear distribution of fine-linear or fine-linear branching (casting) calcifications
    • Irregular hypoechoic mass with spiculation, posterior shadowing, and angular margins on ultrasound

    Management: Tissue sampling (needle biopsy prior to definitive surgical management) Malignancy Risk: ≥95%

  • Category 6: Known Biopsy-Proven Malignancy

    Confirmed Malignancy

    Used for imaging following biopsy that has shown malignancy, performed prior to definitive therapy.

    Use cases:

    • Pre-surgical planning imaging
    • Monitoring response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy
    • Post-biopsy MRI for extent of disease

    Management: Surgical/oncologic management Malignancy Risk: Known malignancy

docs.title: Background Parenchymal Enhancement (BPE)
docs.text:
MRI — Background Parenchymal Enhancement Categories

Category Description Minimal <25% of glandular tissue enhances Mild 25–50% enhances Moderate 50–75% enhances Marked >75% enhances

Moderate/marked BPE can obscure lesions; consider timing relative to menstrual cycle (days 7–14 preferred).

docs.title: Breast Density Categories (Mammography)
docs.text:
Category Description Clinical Significance A Almost entirely fatty Lowest sensitivity limitation B Scattered areas of fibroglandular density Mild sensitivity limitation C Heterogeneously dense May obscure small masses; supplemental screening may be appropriate D Extremely dense Highest sensitivity limitation; supplemental screening recommended

Dense Breast Reporting

Many states require notification letters to patients with heterogeneously dense (C) or extremely dense (D) breasts regarding potential need for supplemental screening.

docs.title: Common Pitfalls
docs.text:
Avoid These Mistakes

❌ Don't:

  • Assign BI-RADS 3 to a screening mammogram without additional workup (category 0 should be assigned first)
  • Use BI-RADS 3 for lesions with >2% malignancy risk features
  • Forget to subcategorize BI-RADS 4 (4A/4B/4C)
  • Apply BI-RADS 6 before tissue sampling has confirmed malignancy

✅ Do:

  • Complete diagnostic workup before assigning BI-RADS 3
  • Correlate mammography and ultrasound findings
  • Describe the most suspicious lesion when multiple are present
  • Include density assessment in every mammography report

docs.title: ACR Contrast Safety — Iodinated & Gadolinium Contrast Agents
docs.text:
Reference Information

Full Name: ACR Manual on Contrast Media Version: 2023 (v11) Publisher: American College of Radiology (ACR) Committee on Drugs and Contrast Media Application: All contrast-enhanced CT, MRI, and fluoroscopic studies Last Institutional Review: January 2024 Modifications: None

Emergency Reference

For acute contrast reactions: see Acute Reaction Treatment below.

docs.title: Adverse Reaction Classification
docs.text:
Acute Reactions (within 1 hour)Delayed Reactions (1–72 hours)Late Reactions (>72 hours) Severity Symptoms Treatment Mild Limited urticaria, nausea/vomiting, pruritus, pallor/diaphoresis Observation, diphenhydramine if hives are bothersome Moderate Diffuse urticaria, mild bronchospasm, facial edema, tachycardia/bradycardia Diphenhydramine, consider epinephrine if bronchospasm Severe Diffuse bronchospasm, laryngeal edema, anaphylactic shock, cardiac arrest Epinephrine (IM), oxygen, IV fluids, call emergency services
  • Predominantly skin reactions (maculopapular rash, urticaria)
  • Usually self-limited; treat with oral antihistamines or steroids if needed
  • Incidence: ~2% after ionic, ~1% after non-ionic contrast
  • Thyroid dysfunction (contrast-induced hyperthyroidism) — especially with high iodine load
  • Metformin-related lactic acidosis risk

docs.title: Acute Contrast Reaction Treatment
docs.text:
Acute Reaction Management Protocol

Urticaria (Mild)BronchospasmLaryngeal EdemaAnaphylaxis / Anaphylactic ShockVasovagal Reaction
  1. Observe patient
  2. If symptomatic: Diphenhydramine 25–50 mg IV/IM
  3. If extensive: consider escalation
  1. Oxygen via face mask (6–10 L/min)
  2. Albuterol (beta-2 agonist) inhaler — 2–3 puffs; may repeat
  3. If no improvement or severe: Epinephrine 0.3 mg IM (1:1,000) in anterolateral thigh
  4. Call emergency services if severe
  1. Oxygen via face mask
  2. Epinephrine 0.3 mg IM (1:1,000) — may repeat every 5–15 min
  3. Call emergency services immediately
  4. Prepare for possible intubation
  1. Call emergency services (Code Blue)
  2. Epinephrine 0.3–0.5 mg IM (1:1,000) — anterolateral thigh; may repeat every 5–15 min
  3. Supine position (legs elevated if tolerated)
  4. Oxygen via face mask (10–15 L/min)
  5. IV access; normal saline 1–2 L bolus for hypotension
  6. Diphenhydramine 25–50 mg IV (adjunct only, NOT primary treatment)
  7. If refractory: epinephrine infusion, vasopressors
  1. Elevate legs (Trendelenburg position)
  2. Oxygen if needed
  3. IV fluids for hypotension
  4. Atropine 0.6–1 mg IV if persistent bradycardia

docs.title: Indications for Premedication
docs.text:
Who Needs Premedication?

  • Prior moderate or severe acute allergic-like reaction to same class of contrast agent
  • Multiple prior mild reactions to same class
  • History of asthma (increased risk of severe reactions)

No premedication proven to eliminate risk entirely

docs.title: Standard Premedication Regimens
docs.text:
Elective (13-hour)Alternative (12-hour)Accelerated (3–5 hour) Drug Dose Timing Methylprednisolone 32 mg PO 13 hours before Methylprednisolone 32 mg PO 1 hour before Diphenhydramine (optional) 50 mg PO/IM/IV 1 hour before Drug Dose Timing Prednisone 50 mg PO 13 hours before Prednisone 50 mg PO 7 hours before Prednisone 50 mg PO 1 hour before Diphenhydramine (optional) 50 mg PO 1 hour before Drug Dose Timing Methylprednisolone 40 mg IV Every 4 hours until contrast Diphenhydramine 50 mg IV 1 hour before

Accelerated regimens are less effective — use only when 12-hour regimen is not feasible

docs.title: Contrast-Induced Acute Kidney Injury (CI-AKI)
docs.text:
Risk Stratification for CI-AKI

True CI-AKI is less common than historically believed. Risk is primarily significant in patients with severely reduced GFR.

eGFR (mL/min/1.73m²) Risk Level Recommendation ≥45 Low Contrast may be given; no additional precautions 30–44 Moderate IV hydration if feasible; consider benefit vs. risk <30 (not on dialysis) Elevated Use with caution; discuss with ordering physician; IV hydration Dialysis-dependent N/A Contrast may be given; coordinate dialysis timing if possible IV Hydration ProtocolN-Acetylcysteine

For patients at risk (eGFR 30–44 or risk factors):

  • Normal saline 100 mL/hr IV — 6 hours before and 6 hours after contrast, OR
  • Sodium bicarbonate 1.26% solution 3 mL/kg/hr × 1 hour before, then 1 mL/kg/hr × 6 hours after (alternative)

Not recommended — evidence does not support use for CI-AKI prevention.

docs.title: Metformin Guidelines
docs.text:
Metformin and Iodinated Contrast

Risk of lactic acidosis is low but can be serious. Current ACR guidance:

eGFR Action ≥30 (intra-arterial or intravenous) No need to hold metformin — low risk in hemodynamically stable patients with eGFR ≥30 <30 OR acute kidney injury expected Hold metformin at time of contrast; do not restart for 48 hours; recheck renal function before resuming Intra-arterial contrast with eGFR 30–60 Consider holding metformin; reassess renal function at 48 hours

docs.title: Agent Risk Classification
docs.text:
NSF Risk Grouping

Nephrogenic Systemic Fibrosis (NSF) is a rare but serious fibrosing disorder in patients with severely reduced renal function.

Group Agents NSF Risk Group I (Highest) Gadodiamide (Omniscan), gadoversetamide (OptiMARK), gadopentetate dimeglumine (Magnevist) High — avoid in eGFR <30 or AKI Group II (Moderate) Gadobenate dimeglumine (MultiHance), gadofosveset (Ablavar) Intermediate — caution in eGFR <30 Group III (Lowest) Gadobutrol (Gadavist), gadoteridol (ProHance), gadoterate meglumine (Dotarem), gadoterate macrocyclic agents Negligible — preferred in at-risk patients

Preferred Agents

Use macrocyclic ionic or non-ionic agents (Group III) when GBCA is required in patients with eGFR <30.

docs.title: GBCA and Renal Function
docs.text: eGFR Recommendation ≥30 Standard doses of any GBCA are acceptable; Group III preferred 15–29 Group III preferred; use lowest effective dose; document risk discussion <15 or dialysis-dependent Risk vs. benefit discussion required; Group III agents; avoid Group I/II AKI Avoid GBCA if possible; if necessary, Group III only

docs.title: GBCA and Special Populations
docs.text:
PregnancyLactationIodinated Contrast in PregnancyIodinated Contrast During Breastfeeding

GBCA in Pregnancy

  • Gadolinium crosses the placenta; association with adverse effects found in some epidemiologic studies
  • Use only when clearly necessary and benefit outweighs risk
  • Document informed consent discussion
  • Prefer lowest effective dose; avoid repeated exposure
  • MRI without contrast is preferred first for pregnant patients

GBCA During Breastfeeding

  • <0.04% of GBCA dose is excreted into breast milk; <1% of that is absorbed by infant
  • ACR guidance: GBCA administration is safe to continue breastfeeding; interruption is not necessary
  • However, if patient requests: may pump-and-discard for 24 hours for peace of mind

ICM in Pregnancy

  • Iodinated contrast crosses the placenta; neonatal thyroid function may be transiently suppressed
  • Use only when clinically necessary
  • If administered: check neonatal thyroid function in first week after delivery
  • <1% of dose enters breast milk; <1% of that is absorbed
  • ACR guidance: Continue breastfeeding after iodinated contrast without interruption

docs.title: Contrast Extravasation
docs.text:
Extravasation Management

Severity Volume Presentation Management Minor <10 mL of iso-osmolar or <2 mL of high-osmolar Minimal pain/swelling Elevate extremity, ice packs 15–20 min several times/day; monitor Significant >10 mL of iso-osmolar or >2 mL of high-osmolar; hypo-osmolar agents; any volume with tissue ischemia symptoms Marked swelling, blistering, vascular compromise Elevate extremity, plastic surgery / wound care consultation; imaging if vascular compromise suspected

Follow-up contact: Advise patients to return if symptoms worsen, paresthesia, or skin changes develop.

docs.title: Thyroid and Iodinated Contrast
docs.text:
Iodinated Contrast and Thyroid Disease

  • Large doses of iodinated contrast can cause contrast-induced thyrotoxicosis in susceptible patients (autonomous nodules, Graves' disease)
  • Patients with known hyperthyroidism or autonomous nodules: discuss with ordering physician and endocrinology prior to administering high-dose iodinated contrast
  • Routine patients without known thyroid disease: no special precautions needed
  • Contrast may interfere with radioiodine therapy — delay radioactive iodine treatment by 2–6 months after large contrast doses

docs.title: Contrast Reaction Risk Factors
docs.text: Factor Increased Risk (ICM) Increased Risk (GBCA) Prior reaction (same class) Yes — major risk factor Yes Asthma 2–3× increased risk Slightly increased Multiple allergies/atopy Yes Yes Cardiac disease For severe reactions No Renal impairment CI-AKI risk NSF risk Anxiety Yes (idiosyncratic) No Beta-blocker use May worsen reaction, impairs epinephrine response No

docs.title: Common Pitfalls
docs.text:
Avoid These Mistakes

❌ Don't:

  • Treat contrast reactions with antihistamines alone — epinephrine is first-line for anaphylaxis
  • Use Group I/II GBCA agents in patients with eGFR <30 when alternatives exist
  • Delay epinephrine waiting for IV access — give IM first
  • Routinely hold metformin for eGFR ≥30 (outdated practice)

✅ Do:

  • Have emergency medications and equipment immediately available in all contrast-administering areas
  • Check eGFR and relevant medications before contrast
  • Document contrast type, dose, lot number, and any reactions in the medical record
  • Choose macrocyclic GBCA when renal function is compromised
  • Brief all imaging staff on acute reaction protocols

docs.title: Emergency Equipment Checklist
docs.text:
Required Emergency Supplies (Contrast Area)
  • Epinephrine 1:1,000 (1 mg/mL) for IM injection
  • Oxygen supply and face masks
  • IV access supplies
  • Normal saline (1 L bags)
  • Diphenhydramine (IV/IM)
  • Atropine
  • Albuterol inhaler
  • Blood pressure cuff and pulse oximeter
  • Crash cart accessible within minutes

docs.title: Bosniak Classification of Renal Cystic Masses
docs.text:
Reference Information

Full Citation: Silverman SG, Pedrosa I, Ellis JH, et al. Bosniak Classification of Cystic Renal Masses, Version 2019: An Update Proposal and Needs Assessment. Radiology. 2019;292(2):475–488. Version: v2019 Update Modality: CT and MRI Last Institutional Review: January 2024 Modifications: None — following published v2019 criteria

About the Bosniak Classification

The Bosniak classification categorises incidentally detected renal cystic masses by their imaging features to predict malignancy risk. Version 2019 refined criteria to improve reproducibility using both CT and MRI.

docs.title: Bosniak Calculator
docs.text: Enhancement None — no enhancement Perceived — equivocal / <20 HU change Measurable — definite ≥20 HU change Wall / Septa Morphology Thin smooth (hairline, <2 mm) Smooth thickened (≥4 mm, regular) Irregular / nodular / thick irregular Number of Septa (non-enhancing lesions) None 1–3 septa 4 or more septa Calcification None Fine / thin Thick / nodular / irregular Non-enhancing High Attenuation (>20 HU on unenhanced CT) No Yes — homogeneous high-density non-enhancing cyst Lesion Size (cm) — required for high-attenuation cysts Calculate Bosniak Category

docs.title: Bosniak Category Summary
docs.text: Categories I–II (Benign)Category IIF (Follow-up)Categories III–IV (Surgical) Category Features Malignancy Risk Management I Simple cyst: hairline-thin wall, no septa, calcification, or solid components, water density <1% No follow-up II Minimally complex: ≤3 hairline-thin septa, fine calcification, or non-enhancing high-attenuation ≤3 cm ~2% No follow-up Criteria Malignancy Risk Management ≥4 hairline-thin septa ~5–15% CT/MRI at 6 mo, then annually ×5 yr Minimally thickened smooth septa/wall ~5–15% CT/MRI at 6 mo, then annually ×5 yr Thick or nodular calcification (no enhancement) ~5–15% CT/MRI at 6 mo, then annually ×5 yr Non-enhancing high-attenuation cyst >3 cm ~5–15% CT/MRI at 6 mo, then annually ×5 yr Perceived (equivocal) enhancement ~5–15% CT/MRI at 6 mo, then annually ×5 yr Category Features Malignancy Risk Management III Enhancing smooth thick (≥4 mm) wall or septa, no solid tissue ~50% Urology referral; surgical excision or active surveillance in selected patients IV Enhancing irregular/nodular wall or septa, OR any enhancing solid tissue >75% Surgical excision or ablation; malignant until proven otherwise

docs.title: Category Descriptions
docs.text:
• Bosniak I — Simple Cyst

    Hairline-thin smooth wall. No septa, calcification, or solid components. Homogeneous water density/signal. No enhancement. Risk <1%. No follow-up required.

  • Bosniak II — Minimally Complex

    1–3 hairline-thin smooth septa (no enhancement, or perceived). Fine calcification. Uniformly high-attenuation ≤3 cm without enhancement. Risk ~2%. No follow-up required.

  • Bosniak IIF — Follow-up Required

    Moderately complex. ≥4 thin septa, minimally thickened smooth wall/septa, thick/nodular calcification, non-enhancing high-attenuation >3 cm, or perceived enhancement. Risk ~5–15%. 6-month then annual CT/MRI for 5 years.

  • Bosniak III — Indeterminate

    Enhancing thick smooth wall or septa. No discrete solid tissue. Risk ~50%. Urology referral; often surgical excision.

  • Bosniak IV — Malignant

    Enhancing irregular/nodular wall or septa, or enhancing solid tissue component. Risk >75%. Surgical excision or ablation.

docs.title: Enhancement Assessment
docs.text:
Measuring Enhancement on CT

Enhancement on CT is defined as an increase of ≥20 HU between unenhanced and contrast-enhanced phases (same region of interest).

  • Measurable (definite): ≥20 HU increase — treat as enhancing
  • Perceived (equivocal): Apparent enhancement but <20 HU increase — category IIF
  • Pseudoenhancement: Common in small intrarenal cysts due to beam hardening — do not upgrade based on pseudoenhancement alone

On MRI, enhancement is assessed qualitatively. Any definite enhancement on subtraction images or post-contrast sequences qualifies as measurable.

docs.title: Special Considerations
docs.text:
Incidentally Detected vs. Known Lesions

These criteria apply to incidentally detected renal cystic masses. Known malignant lesions or lesions in patients with von Hippel-Lindau, tuberous sclerosis, or prior RCC require individualised management.

Active Surveillance for Category III

Active surveillance (repeat imaging without intervention) may be appropriate for Bosniak III lesions in: - Elderly or frail patients with high surgical risk - Bilateral lesions or solitary kidney - Lesion stability on serial imaging

Discuss risk-benefit with urology and patient.

docs.title: Malignancy Rates Summary
docs.text:
Category Approximate Malignancy Rate Common Pathology I <1% Simple cyst II ~2% Benign cyst IIF ~5–15% Benign or low-grade RCC III ~50% Clear cell RCC, multilocular cystic RCC IV >75% Clear cell RCC, papillary RCC

Rates based on pooled surgical series; individual risk depends on patient factors.

docs.title: PI-RADS v2.1 — Prostate MRI Reporting & Data System
docs.text:
Reference Information

Full Name: Prostate Imaging Reporting and Data System Version: 2.1 (2019) Publisher: American College of Radiology (ACR) / European Society of Urogenital Radiology (ESUR) Application: Multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) of the prostate Last Institutional Review: January 2024 Modifications: None

Purpose

PI-RADS v2.1 standardizes mpMRI assessment for clinically significant prostate cancer (csPCa), defined as Gleason score ≥ 7 (Grade Group ≥ 2) or tumor volume ≥ 0.5 cc.

docs.title: PI-RADS Calculator
docs.text: Lesion Zone Peripheral Zone (PZ) — DWI dominant Transition Zone (TZ) — T2WI dominant DWI Score (1–5) — Primary for PZ; secondary for TZ 1 — No abnormality 2 — Indistinct hypointensity on ADC 3 — Focal mild/moderate hypointensity; <1.5 cm 4 — Focal marked hypointensity; <1.5 cm 5 — As 4 but ≥1.5 cm or definite EPE T2WI Score (1–5) — Primary for TZ; not used for PZ scoring 1 — Normal TZ or round well-defined BPH nodule 2 — Circumscribed hypointense, well-defined margins 3 — Heterogeneous, obscured margins 4 — Lenticular/non-circumscribed homogeneous; <1.5 cm 5 — As 4 but ≥1.5 cm or definite EPE DCE Positive? — PZ only: focal early enhancement vs. adjacent tissue No (or not applicable) Yes — Focal early enhancement Calculate PI-RADS PI-RADS Quick Reference Score Probability of csPCa Action PI-RADS 1 Very low (essentially no csPCa) No biopsy PI-RADS 2 Low No biopsy (consider in context) PI-RADS 3 Intermediate Biopsy at clinician discretion (consider PSA density, clinical risk) PI-RADS 4 High Targeted biopsy recommended PI-RADS 5 Very high Targeted biopsy strongly recommended

docs.title: MRI Sequences and Dominant Sequence by Zone
docs.text:
Dominant Sequence by Zone

Zone Dominant Sequence Secondary Sequence Peripheral Zone (PZ) DWI (Diffusion-Weighted Imaging) DCE (Dynamic Contrast Enhancement) Transition Zone (TZ) T2WI (T2-Weighted Imaging) DWI Central Zone Assessed as part of TZ —

docs.title: Peripheral Zone (PZ) — DWI Dominant
docs.text:
DWI Scores (PZ)DCE Role (PZ) DWI Score Description 1 No abnormality (homogeneous hyperintense on ADC and iso/hyperintense on high b-value DWI) 2 Indistinct hypointense on ADC 3 Focal mildly/moderately hypointense on ADC and iso/mildly hyperintense on high b-value DWI; <1.5 cm 4 Focal markedly hypointense on ADC and markedly hyperintense on high b-value DWI; <1.5 cm 5 As per 4 but ≥1.5 cm, or definite extraprostatic extension/invasive behavior

DCE is used as a secondary sequence in the PZ:

  • DCE positive: Focal early enhancement (earlier than or simultaneously with adjacent normal tissue), used to upgrade DWI score 3 → PI-RADS 4
  • DCE negative: Does not change the DWI assessment

DCE Upgrade Rule

PZ DWI 3 + DCE positive → PI-RADS 4 PZ DWI 3 + DCE negative → PI-RADS 3

docs.title: Transition Zone (TZ) — T2WI Dominant
docs.text:
T2WI Scores (TZ)DWI Role (TZ) T2WI Score Description 1 Normal-appearing TZ (homogeneous intermediate signal); or round/well-defined hypointense nodule (BPH nodule) 2 Circumscribed hypointense or heterogeneous signal intensity but well-defined margins 3 Heterogeneous signal intensity with obscured margins (includes others not qualifying as 2, 4, or 5) 4 Lenticular or non-circumscribed, homogeneous, moderately hypointense; <1.5 cm 5 As per 4 but ≥1.5 cm, or definite extraprostatic extension/invasive behavior

DWI is used as a secondary sequence in the TZ:

  • DWI 4 or 5: Upgrade T2WI 3 → PI-RADS 4
  • DWI 1, 2, or 3: Does not change T2WI assessment

DWI Upgrade Rule

TZ T2WI 3 + DWI ≥4 → PI-RADS 4 TZ T2WI 3 + DWI ≤3 → PI-RADS 3

docs.title: Overall PI-RADS Score Assignment
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A[Identify lesion zone] --> B{PZ or TZ?}
    B -->|Peripheral Zone| C[DWI score 1–5]
    B -->|Transition Zone| D[T2WI score 1–5]

    C -->|DWI = 3| E{DCE positive?}
    E -->|Yes| F[PI-RADS 4]
    E -->|No| G[PI-RADS 3]
    C -->|DWI = 1, 2, 4, or 5| H[PI-RADS = DWI score]

    D -->|T2WI = 3| I{DWI ≥ 4?}
    I -->|Yes| J[PI-RADS 4]
    I -->|No| K[PI-RADS 3]
    D -->|T2WI = 1, 2, 4, or 5| L[PI-RADS = T2WI score]

docs.title: Anatomic Zones of the Prostate
docs.text: Zone Location Proportion of Gland Cancer Origin Peripheral Zone (PZ) Posterior/lateral, base to apex ~70% ~70% of PCa Transition Zone (TZ) Surrounding urethra, central gland ~20–30% ~20–30% of PCa Central Zone (CZ) Surrounding ejaculatory ducts at base ~5% Rare Anterior Fibromuscular Stroma (AFMS) Anterior N/A Rare

docs.title: Extraprostatic Extension (EPE) Assessment
docs.text:
Extraprostatic Extension Indicators

Feature Significance Capsular bulge or irregularity Suspicious for EPE Broad capsular contact (≥1 cm) Higher EPE risk Obliteration of rectoprostatic angle Suspicious for EPE Neurovascular bundle asymmetry Suspicious for involvement Tumor abutting seminal vesicle Seminal vesicle invasion risk

Seminal Vesicle Invasion (SVI)

Low T2/ADC signal within seminal vesicle + direct extension from primary tumor = SVI (T3b disease)

docs.title: PSA Density (PSAD)
docs.text:
\[\text{PSAD} = \frac{\text{PSA (ng/mL)}}{\text{Prostate Volume (cc)}}\] PSAD Interpretation <0.10 Low risk; biopsy may be deferred in PI-RADS 3 0.10–0.15 Intermediate; shared decision-making for PI-RADS 3 ≥0.15 Higher risk; supports biopsy recommendation for PI-RADS 3

Prostate Volume Measurement

Use ellipsoid formula: V = 0.52 × AP × TR × CC (all in cm)

docs.title: PSA Density Calculator
docs.text: PSA (ng/mL) Prostate Volume (cc) — V = 0.52 × AP × TR × CC Calculate PSA Density

docs.title: Technical Requirements for mpMRI
docs.text:
Minimum StandardsField Strength Sequence Requirement T2WI ≥3 planes; slice thickness ≤3 mm; in-plane resolution ≤0.7 mm DWI Include b = 0 and b ≥ 800 s/mm²; calculate ADC map; optional high b-value (b ≥ 1400 s/mm²) DCE Temporal resolution ≤15 sec; spatial coverage of entire prostate
  • 3T preferred — higher SNR and spatial resolution
  • 1.5T acceptable — endorectal coil may improve quality
  • Endorectal coil not required at 3T with modern phased-array surface coils

docs.title: Reporting Template Elements
docs.text:
Required Report Elements

  1. Technique: Field strength, coil type, sequences performed
  2. Prostate: Volume, PSA density (if PSA available), overall gland appearance
  3. Lesion description (for each PI-RADS ≥ 3):
    • Location (zone, sector per 27-sector map)
    • Size (cm)
    • DWI score, T2WI score, DCE result
    • Overall PI-RADS score
    • EPE suspicion
  4. Lymph nodes: Regional nodal assessment
  5. Seminal vesicles
  6. Incidental findings
  7. Overall impression: Most significant PI-RADS score; clinical management recommendation

docs.title: Prostate Sector Map (27-Sector)
docs.text:
Zone Sectors for Localization

Peripheral Zone (18 sectors): - Right/Left × Base/Mid/Apex × Anterior/Posterior/Lateral

Transition Zone (4 sectors): - Right/Left × Base/Mid

Anterior Fibromuscular Stroma (1 sector)

Seminal Vesicles (2 sectors, not numbered)

Note

Use standardized sector map in reports and communicate sector to urology for targeted biopsy planning.

docs.title: Common Pitfalls
docs.text:
Avoid These Mistakes

❌ Don't:

  • Assign PI-RADS based on a single sequence — integrate all sequences
  • Ignore the distinction between PZ and TZ (each has a different dominant sequence)
  • Overcall BPH nodules in TZ as PI-RADS 4/5 — BPH has characteristic encapsulated, heterogeneous appearance
  • Overlook anterior TZ tumors — they can be subtle on DWI
  • Forget EPE assessment for PI-RADS ≥ 4 lesions

✅ Do:

  • Measure prostate volume and calculate PSAD when PSA is available
  • Report sector location for targeted biopsy planning
  • Note prior treatment (radiation, HIFU, focal ablation) — may alter assessment
  • Compare to any prior MRI

docs.title: LI-RADS — Liver Imaging Reporting and Data System
docs.text:
Reference Information

Full Citation: American College of Radiology. CT/MRI LI-RADS v2018. ACR LI-RADS® Working Group. 2018. Version: v2018 (CT/MRI) Last Institutional Review: January 2024 Modifications: None — following published algorithm exactly Applicability: Patients at risk for HCC: cirrhosis, chronic HBV infection, or personal history of HCC

At-Risk Patients Only

LI-RADS applies only to patients at risk for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC): cirrhosis (any cause), chronic hepatitis B virus (HBV) infection, or prior HCC. For observations in non-at-risk patients, standard reporting categories apply.

docs.title: LI-RADS Calculator
docs.text: Observation Type No definite/probable benign features — apply major criteria Definitely benign — cyst, haemangioma, vascular anomaly (LR-1) Probably benign — no malignant features (LR-2) Observation Size (mm) Non-rim Arterial Phase Hyperenhancement (APHE) No / Absent / Rim APHE Yes — Non-rim APHE present Non-peripheral Washout Appearance No Yes Enhancing Capsule Appearance No Yes Threshold Growth (≥50% increase ≤6 months) No / Unknown Yes LR-M Features (targetoid, infiltrative, non-HCC malignancy) No Yes — features suggest non-HCC malignancy Definite Tumor in Vein (LR-TIV) No Yes — definite tumor thrombus Calculate LI-RADS Category

docs.title: Quick Reference — Major Features
docs.text: Major Feature Definition Non-rim APHE Arterial phase enhancement greater than liver, non-rim pattern Washout appearance Hypointensity/hypoattenuation on portal venous or delayed phase relative to liver Enhancing capsule Smooth peripheral enhancement on portal venous or delayed phase Threshold growth ≥50% diameter increase in ≤6 months, or ≥50% increase when interval uncertain

docs.title: LI-RADS Category Table (CT/MRI)
docs.text:
Main AlgorithmSpecial Categories Size APHE Additional Major Features Category <10 mm Absent 0–1 LR-3 <10 mm Absent ≥2 LR-4 <10 mm Present 0 LR-3 <10 mm Present ≥1 LR-4 10–19 mm Absent 0 LR-3 10–19 mm Absent ≥1 LR-4 10–19 mm Present 0 LR-4 10–19 mm Present ≥1 LR-5 ≥20 mm Absent 0 LR-3 ≥20 mm Absent ≥1 LR-4 ≥20 mm Present 0 LR-4 ≥20 mm Present ≥1 LR-5

Additional major features = washout + enhancing capsule + threshold growth (count of present features)

Category HCC Risk Criteria Management LR-1 ~0% Definitely benign: cyst, haemangioma, vascular anomaly, hepatic fat, post-treatment complete response No HCC workup; routine surveillance if at-risk LR-2 <10% Probably benign: no malignant features, likely benign entity, or small (<10 mm) indeterminate Routine HCC surveillance only LR-TIV N/A Definite tumor in vein (neoplastic thrombus) Multidisciplinary review; staging and treatment LR-M N/A Probably/definitely malignant, not HCC-specific (targetoid, infiltrative) Biopsy to determine malignancy type

docs.title: Category Descriptions
docs.text:
• LR-1 — Definitely Benign (~0% HCC risk)

    Imaging features diagnostic of a benign entity: definite cyst, definite haemangioma, vascular anomaly/shunt, perfusion alteration, hepatic fat deposition/sparing, post-treatment change showing complete response, or other definitely benign diagnosis. No HCC workup required. Continue routine surveillance if patient remains at risk.

  • LR-2 — Probably Benign (<10% HCC risk)

    Probably benign — does not meet LR-1 criteria but has no features suggesting malignancy. Includes small (<10 mm) observations with no malignant features or observations that probably represent a benign entity. Continue routine HCC surveillance. No additional workup required.

  • LR-3 — Intermediate (~38% HCC risk)

    Intermediate probability of malignancy. Continue routine HCC surveillance. Follow-up imaging in 3–6 months or multidisciplinary review.

  • LR-4 — Probably HCC (~74% HCC risk)

    Probably HCC. Multidisciplinary discussion recommended. Options: alternative modality imaging (MRI if CT), biopsy, or proceed to treatment if clinical suspicion high.

  • LR-5 — Definitely HCC (≥95% HCC risk)

    Definite HCC — no biopsy required if imaging criteria met. Refer for HCC treatment: resection, transplantation, ablation (RFA/MWA), or TACE depending on BCLC staging and liver function.

  • LR-M — Non-HCC Malignancy

    Features suggest malignancy but not HCC-specific (e.g., intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma). Biopsy recommended to characterise malignancy type.

  • LR-TIV — Tumor in Vein

    Definite neoplastic thrombus. Associated with advanced-stage HCC. Multidisciplinary team review required for staging and treatment planning.

docs.title: LR-M Features
docs.text:
Features that suggest non-HCC malignancy and should prompt LR-M categorisation:

  • Targetoid appearance — rim APHE, peripheral washout, delayed central enhancement, peripheral diffusion restriction
  • Infiltrative appearance — ill-defined margins, geographic shape
  • Marked diffusion restriction disproportionate to T2 signal
  • Necrosis, satellite nodules adjacent to a mass
  • Other features — biliary obstruction, vascular invasion without TIV

docs.title: BCLC Staging & Treatment Reference
docs.text:
Treatment depends on BCLC Stage

LI-RADS categorises the observation — treatment selection uses BCLC staging, which incorporates tumour burden, liver function (Child-Pugh), and performance status.

BCLC Stage Tumour Liver Function Treatment Options 0 (Very early) Single ≤2 cm Child-Pugh A Resection or ablation A (Early) 1–3 nodules ≤3 cm or single any size Child-Pugh A–B Resection, transplant, or ablation B (Intermediate) Multinodular, no vascular invasion Child-Pugh A–B TACE C (Advanced) Vascular invasion or extrahepatic spread Child-Pugh A–B Systemic therapy (atezolizumab + bevacizumab) D (Terminal) Any Child-Pugh C Best supportive care

docs.title: Special Considerations
docs.text:
Ancillary Features

LI-RADS v2018 allows upgrading or downgrading by one category based on ancillary features:

  • Upgrade features (suggest HCC): Restricted diffusion, T1 hypointensity, T2 mild hyperintensity, transitional/hepatobiliary phase hypointensity, fat in mass
  • Downgrade features (suggest benign): Definite intralesional fat, blood products, intralesional gas, lesion stability ≥2 years

Imaging Protocol Requirements

Adequate CT requires: unenhanced, late arterial, portal venous, and delayed phases. Adequate MRI requires: T2, DWI, unenhanced T1, late arterial, portal venous, delayed, and hepatobiliary phase (if hepatobiliary agent used).

docs.title: Fleischner Society Guidelines for Incidental Pulmonary Nodules
docs.text:
Reference Information

Full Citation: MacMahon H, Naidich DP, Goo JM, et al. Guidelines for Management of Incidental Pulmonary Nodules Detected on CT Images: From the Fleischner Society 2017. Radiology. 2017;284(1):228-243. Version: 2017 Update Last Institutional Review: January 2024 Modifications: None - following published guidelines exactly

docs.title: Fleischner Calculator
docs.text: Nodule Size (mm) Nodule Type Solid Part-Solid Ground-Glass Multiplicity Single Multiple Patient Risk Low Risk High Risk Calculate Recommendation

docs.title: Quick Reference Tables
docs.text:
Solid nodulesSubsolid nodules Nodule type Risk <6 mm 6–8 mm >8 mm Comments Single Low Risk No routine follow-up CT 6–12 mo; consider CT 18–24 mo Consider CT ~3 mo, PET/CT and/or biopsy Consider 12 mo FU in selected suspicious nodules Single High Risk Optional CT at 12 mo CT 6–12 mo and CT 18–24 mo Consider CT ~3 mo, PET/CT and/or biopsy Higher concern with suspicious morphology / upper lobe, etc. Multiple Low Risk No routine follow-up CT 3–6 mo; consider CT 18–24 mo CT 3–6 mo ± PET/CT/biopsy Base management on most suspicious nodule Multiple High Risk Optional CT at 12 mo CT 3–6 mo and CT 18–24 mo CT 3–6 mo ± PET/CT/biopsy Base management on most suspicious nodule

Perifissural note: If a nodule is perifissural with a polygonal shape (typical intrapulmonary lymph node), no follow-up is required.

Nodule type Pattern <6 mm ≥6 mm Comments Single Ground-glass No routine follow-up CT 6–12 mo to confirm persistence, then q2y until 5y Consider longer follow-up if suspicious Single Part-solid No routine follow-up CT 3–6 mo to confirm persistence; if persistent, annual CT until 5y (manage by solid component) Persistent part-solid often higher risk Multiple Ground-glass CT 3–6 mo; if stable, consider CT at 2 and 4y CT 3–6 mo; subsequent management guided by most suspicious nodule Follow most suspicious nodule Multiple Part-solid CT 3–6 mo; if stable, consider CT at 2 and 4y CT 3–6 mo; subsequent management guided by most suspicious nodule Follow most suspicious nodule

docs.title: Risk Criteria
docs.text:
• Low Risk Criteria

    • Minimal or absent smoking history
    • No other risk factors

  • High Risk Criteria

    • Current or former smoker
    • Family history of lung cancer
    • Radon or asbestos exposure
    • Age >50 years
    • COPD or pulmonary fibrosis
How to Measure Nodules

Solid Nodules:

  • Measure longest diameter in axial plane
  • Use lung windows (W: 1500, L: -600)
  • Average diameter if growth assessment needed

Part-Solid Nodules:

  • Measure total diameter (entire nodule)
  • Measure solid component separately
  • Solid component determines management

Ground Glass Nodules:

  • Measure longest diameter
  • Volume measurement preferred when available

Preferred Method:

  • Semi-automated volumetry when available
  • Volume doubling time (VDT) for growth assessment

docs.title: When to Use Lung-RADS Instead
docs.text:
Use Lung-RADS for Screening Exams

These Fleischner guidelines apply to incidental nodules found on CT performed for other reasons.

For lung cancer screening CTs, use Lung-RADS instead.

docs.title: Lung-RADS™ v1.1 - Lung Cancer Screening Reporting
docs.text:
Reference Information

Full Name: Lung CT Screening Reporting & Data System Version: 1.1 (2019) Publisher: American College of Radiology (ACR) Application: Annual low-dose CT lung cancer screening Last Institutional Review: January 2024 Modifications: None

Lung-RADS Quick Reference Category Finding Management Malignancy Risk Lung-RADS 0 Incomplete Additional imaging needed N/A Lung-RADS 1 Negative Continue annual screening <1% Lung-RADS 2 Benign Continue annual screening <1% Lung-RADS 3 Probably benign 6-month follow-up CT 1-2% Lung-RADS 4A Suspicious 3-month follow-up CT 5-15% Lung-RADS 4B Very suspicious 3-month PET/CT or tissue sampling >15% Lung-RADS 4X Category 3 or 4 + additional findings See category 3 or 4 management Variable

docs.title: Lung-RADS Calculator
docs.text: Nodule Type Solid Part-Solid Ground-Glass (Non-Solid) Total Nodule Diameter (mm) Solid Component Diameter (mm) — Part-solid only; leave blank if none New nodule on this exam? (not present on prior CT) No — Baseline or previously seen Yes — New nodule Additional suspicious features? (spiculation, lymphadenopathy, endobronchial lesion) No Yes — Apply 4X modifier Calculate Lung-RADS

docs.title: Detailed Classification
docs.text:
• Category 0: Incomplete

    Additional Imaging Needed

    Reasons for Category 0:

    • Prior CT needed for comparison
    • Technically inadequate study
    • Additional imaging recommended (e.g., dedicated chest CT for findings on non-screening CT)

    Management: Complete imaging before final classification

  • Category 1: Negative

    No Nodules - Annual Screening

    Findings:

    • No lung nodules
    • Definitely benign nodules only (completely calcified, fat-containing)

    Management: Continue annual LDCT screening in 12 months

    Malignancy Risk: <1%

  • Category 2: Benign Appearance or Behavior

    Benign - Annual Screening

    Nodules with specific benign features:

    • Perifissural or perivascular nodules <10 mm
    • Completely or centrally calcified nodules
    • Fat-containing nodules

    Size criteria (no suspicious features):

    • Solid: <6 mm average diameter
    • Part-solid: <6 mm total diameter
    • Ground glass (non-solid): <30 mm

    Management: Continue annual LDCT in 12 months

    Malignancy Risk: <1%

  • Category 3: Probably Benign

    Short-Term Follow-Up Recommended

    Solid nodules:

    • ≥6 to <8 mm average diameter

    Part-solid nodules:

    • ≥6 mm total diameter with solid component <6 mm

    Ground glass (non-solid):

    • ≥30 mm

    New nodules (compared to prior):

    • <4 mm

    Management: 6-month LDCT

    Malignancy Risk: 1-2%

  • Category 4A: Suspicious

    Suspicious - Consider PET/CT or Biopsy

    Solid nodules:

    • ≥8 to <15 mm average diameter

    Part-solid nodules:

    • ≥6 mm total diameter with solid component ≥6 to <8 mm

    Ground glass (non-solid):

    • No specific size criteria (determined by radiologist judgment)

    New nodules:

    • ≥4 to <6 mm

    Management: 3-month LDCT

    Malignancy Risk: 5-15%

  • Category 4B: Very Suspicious

    Very Suspicious - PET/CT, Tissue Sampling, or Surgical Consultation

    Solid nodules:

    • ≥15 mm average diameter

    Part-solid nodules:

    • ≥6 mm total diameter with solid component ≥8 mm

    Ground glass (non-solid):

    • No specific size criteria (determined by radiologist judgment)

    New nodules:

    • ≥6 mm

    Category 3 or 4A nodules with growth:

    • Growth on interval imaging

    Management: Chest CT with or without contrast, PET/CT, and/or tissue sampling depending on probability of malignancy and comorbidities

    Malignancy Risk: >15%

  • Category 4X: Additional Suspicious Features

    Category 3 or 4 + Clinically Significant Findings

    Applies when Category 3 or 4 nodule PLUS:

    • Lymphadenopathy suspicious for lung cancer
    • Suspicious lung mass/nodule features (spiculation, cavity)
    • Endobronchial lesion

    Management: Consider chest CT with contrast, PET/CT, and/or tissue sampling

    Malignancy Risk: Variable (based on underlying category + additional features)

docs.title: Measurement Guidelines
docs.text:
How to Measure - Lung-RADS Specific

Solid Nodules:

  • Measure average diameter: (longest diameter + perpendicular diameter) ÷ 2
  • Use lung windows (W: 1500, L: -600)
  • Round to nearest whole millimeter

Part-Solid Nodules:

  • Measure total diameter (entire nodule including ground glass)
  • Measure solid component separately using average diameter
  • Solid component determines category

Ground Glass (Non-Solid) Nodules:

  • Measure average diameter
  • Must obscure vessels but no solid component

Multiple Nodules:

  • Classify based on most suspicious nodule
  • Report up to 3 most significant nodules

docs.title: S Modifier: Significant or Potentially Significant Findings
docs.text:
Add "S" modifier for clinically significant findings unrelated to lung cancer:

  • Coronary artery calcification (specify if severe)
  • Emphysema (specify if severe)
  • Interstitial lung abnormalities
  • Infectious/inflammatory conditions requiring treatment

Example: Lung-RADS 2S (benign nodule + severe emphysema)

docs.title: C Modifier: Previously Known Cancer
docs.text:
Not used in standard lung cancer screening

Reserved for incidental CTs in patients with known cancer.

docs.title: Comparison to Prior Exams
docs.text:
New vs Stable vs Growing Nodules

Baseline Exam (No Prior):

  • Classify based on size criteria above

Subsequent Exams (With Prior):

  • Stable nodule: Remains same category or improves → Continue as per category
  • New nodule: Not present on prior → Use "new nodule" size criteria
  • Growing nodule: ≥1.5 mm increase in average diameter → Lung-RADS 4B

Volume Doubling Time (VDT):

  • VDT <400 days = concerning for malignancy

docs.title: Lung Cancer Screening Eligibility
docs.text:
Who Should Be Screened?

USPSTF 2021 Criteria:

  • Age 50-80 years
  • ≥20 pack-year smoking history
  • Current smoker OR quit within past 15 years
  • No signs/symptoms of lung cancer

Shared Decision-Making Required

When to Stop Screening:

  • Age >80 years
  • Comorbidities limiting life expectancy
  • Patient preference

docs.title: External Links
docs.text:
• ACR Lung-RADS Official Page
  • USPSTF Lung Cancer Screening Recommendation
  • National Lung Screening Trial (NLST) Results

docs.title: Related Guidelines
docs.text:
• Fleischner Society 2017 - For incidental nodules
  • NCCN Lung Cancer Screening Guidelines

docs.title: Common Pitfalls
docs.text:
Avoid These Mistakes

❌ Don't:

  • Use Lung-RADS for incidental nodules (use Fleischner instead)
  • Measure nodules in coronal/sagittal only (use axial)
  • Ignore new nodules <4 mm (still report them)
  • Apply to patients outside screening age range

✅ Do:

  • Always compare to prior exams when available
  • Measure solid component of part-solid nodules
  • Document smoking history in report
  • Communicate high-risk findings (4B, 4X) directly to ordering provider

docs.title: Detailed Classification
docs.text:
• TR1: Benign

    No Action Required

    No sonographic features of malignancy. Essentially benign nodule.

    Typical findings:

    • Simple cysts (completely anechoic, no solid component)
    • Spongiform nodules (>50% microcystic composition)

    Management: No FNA, no follow-up Malignancy Risk: ~0.3% Points: 0

  • TR2: Not Suspicious

    No Action Required

    Not suspicious. Sonographic features suggest a benign etiology.

    Typical findings:

    • Predominantly cystic with thin internal septa
    • Isoechoic or hyperechoic nodules without suspicious features
    • Colloid nodules with comet-tail artifacts

    Management: No FNA, no follow-up Malignancy Risk: ~1.5% Points: 2

  • TR3: Mildly Suspicious

    Size-Based Follow-up

    Mildly suspicious features. Management is determined by size thresholds.

    FNA if: ≥2.5 cm Follow-up if: ≥1.5 cm (at 1, 3, 5 years) No action if: <1.5 cm

    Management: Observation or FNA based on size Malignancy Risk: ~4.8% Points: 3

  • TR4: Moderately Suspicious

    Biopsy Consideration

    Moderately suspicious features. Lower size thresholds than TR3.

    FNA if: ≥1.5 cm Follow-up if: ≥1.0 cm (at 1, 2, 3, 5 years) No action if: <1.0 cm

    Management: Follow-up or FNA based on size Malignancy Risk: ~9.1% Points: 4–6

  • TR5: Highly Suspicious

    FNA Recommended

    Highly suspicious features. FNA is recommended above size threshold.

    FNA if: ≥1.0 cm Follow-up if: ≥0.5 cm (at 1, 2, 3, 5 years) No action if: <0.5 cm

    Management: FNA recommended Malignancy Risk: ~35% Points: ≥7

docs.title: Feature Scoring Reference
docs.text:
TI-RADS Scoring Feature Points Description COMPOSITION Cystic or almost completely cystic 0 Anechoic nodule with echogenic wall ± artifacts Spongiform 0 Aggregation of microcysts >50% of nodule Mixed cystic and solid 1 Any solid component <50% Solid or almost solid 2 Solid component ≥50% ECHOGENICITY Anechoic 0 Simple cyst appearance Hyperechoic or isoechoic 1 Same or brighter than thyroid Hypoechoic 2 Darker than thyroid Very hypoechoic 3 Darker than strap muscles SHAPE Wider-than-tall 0 A-P < transverse diameter Taller-than-wide 3 A-P ≥ transverse diameter on transverse view MARGIN Smooth 0 Well-defined smooth border Ill-defined 0 Cannot clearly define margin Lobulated or irregular 2 Undulating or jagged contour Extra-thyroidal extension 3 Extends beyond thyroid capsule ECHOGENIC FOCI None or large comet-tail artifacts 0 No foci or V-shaped artifact from colloid Macrocalcifications 1 Large calcific foci with shadowing Peripheral (rim) calcifications 2 Calcifications along nodule margin Punctate echogenic foci 3 Tiny bright spots ± shadowing/comet-tail Measurement Guidelines

Size Measurement:

  • Use maximum diameter in any plane
  • Measure in centimeters (to one decimal)
  • Include solid and cystic components

Important Notes:

  • Benign features (TR1-TR2): No size threshold
  • Always measure at largest diameter
  • Document plane of measurement

Growth Assessment:

  • Significant growth: >20% in two dimensions AND >2mm
  • Minimum interval: 6 months
  • Use same measurement technique

docs.title: Special Considerations
docs.text:
When NOT to Use TI-RADS

Do NOT apply TI-RADS to:

  • Nodules in patients <14 years old
  • Nodules with known history of thyroid cancer
  • Lymph nodes
  • Nodules already biopsied or with known cytology
  • Purely cystic nodules (TR1)

Clinical correlation required for:

  • Rapidly growing nodules
  • Symptomatic nodules (hoarseness, dysphagia)
  • Suspicious lymphadenopathy
  • Elevated thyroglobulin
  • PET-avid nodules
Reporting Tips

Structured Report Should Include:

  • Overall thyroid size and echogenicity
  • Number of nodules
  • For each nodule requiring characterization:
    • Location (lobe, upper/mid/lower)
    • Size (3 dimensions)
    • Composition (points)
    • Echogenicity (points)
    • Shape (points)
    • Margin (points)
    • Echogenic foci (points)
    • Total TI-RADS points and level
    • Recommendation based on size and TR level

Example Template:

Right mid lobe nodule measures 1.8 x 1.2 x 1.5 cm:
- Solid composition (2 points)
- Hypoechoic (2 points)
- Wider-than-tall (0 points)
- Irregular margin (2 points)
- Punctate echogenic foci (3 points)
Total: 9 points = TR5

Recommendation: FNA is recommended.

docs.title: Common Pitfalls
docs.text:
Avoid These Errors

Scoring Errors:

  • Don't add multiple features from same category
  • Don't upgrade based on size alone
  • Don't confuse macrocalcifications with punctate foci

Measurement Errors:

  • Don't include halo in measurement
  • Don't measure only transverse plane
  • Don't round up aggressively

Management Errors:

  • Don't FNA nodules below size threshold
  • Don't ignore clinical context
  • Don't apply to cervical lymph nodes
